questions.q: What is the primary difference between client-server and peer-to-peer (P2P) multiplayer architecture?
options: Client-server has lower latency but P2P is cheaper to operate
In client-server, a central server arbitrates all game state; in P2P, players exchange data directly
P2P is always faster than client-server
Client-server requires internet; P2P works on local networks only
questions.explanation: Client-server centralizes authority on a server (prevents cheating, ensures consistency) but adds server costs and latency. P2P reduces latency between players but complicates cheating prevention and synchronization.

questions.q: What does lag compensation (or client-side prediction) achieve in online games?
options: Eliminates network latency entirely
Allows clients to predict and display state ahead of server feedback to reduce perceived latency, then correct when server truth arrives
Speeds up internet connections
Prevents packet loss
questions.explanation: Without prediction, a 100ms latency makes controls feel sluggish. Clients predict movement and send commands; the server validates and corrects any cheating. Players feel responsive while security is maintained.

questions.q: What is a tick rate in multiplayer game servers?
options: The network latency in milliseconds
How many times per second the server updates and broadcasts game state (e.g., 64 ticks/second = 15.6ms updates)
How fast a player's character moves
The number of players on the server
questions.explanation: Tick rate determines state synchronization frequency: higher ticks (128) feel responsive but cost bandwidth and server CPU; lower ticks (32) save resources but feel laggy. Competitive games run 64-128 ticks; casual games often 32 or 10.

questions.q: What problem does server-authoritative movement solve?
options: It makes games run faster locally
It prevents clients from cheating by moving through walls or teleporting without validation
It reduces bandwidth usage
It eliminates input lag
questions.explanation: Authoritative servers validate all position changes: a client claims to move to (100, 100), but the server checks collision and physics before accepting it. Rogues clients can't teleport or clip through terrain.

questions.q: What is interpolation in multiplayer games and when is it used?
options: Blending between last received state and predicted future state for smooth rendering between network updates
Merging multiple physics materials into one
Reducing file size for network transfer
Waiting for all players to respond before updating
questions.explanation: If the server updates at 32 Hz (31ms apart), the client interpolates between received positions each frame for smooth animation. At 60 FPS with 32 tick server, each frame blends two old states.

questions.q: What does extrapolation (or dead reckoning) do in networked games?
options: Predicting future state based on current velocity to fill gaps between server updates
Extracting game data for debugging
Removing old network packets
Calculating bandwidth requirements
questions.explanation: Extrapolation assumes an object continues at its last known velocity until the next update arrives. This works well for smooth motion but fails when objects suddenly change direction, requiring fast server updates or correction.

questions.q: What is hitching or rubber-banding in online games and what causes it?
options: Intentional gameplay mechanic to slow players down
Correcting player position back to server truth after prediction diverged (caused by high latency or bandwidth spikes)
An animation loop that repeats too slowly
Excessive physics sub-stepping
questions.explanation: When network conditions worsen or prediction drifts significantly, the server correction is large and visible: a player teleports back, creating the appearance of being pulled by a rubber band. This happens during lag spikes.

questions.q: In a client-server game, why is input sent to the server rather than actions being executed locally first?
options: To reduce input latency on the client
To allow the server to validate inputs and prevent cheating (e.g., impossible jump), then broadcast to all clients
Because clients are faster than servers
To save bandwidth by sending less data
questions.explanation: Authoritative servers receive raw input (e.g., 'jump'), validate it against game rules, simulate the result, and broadcast the outcome. This prevents clients from claiming success for disallowed actions.

questions.q: What is the difference between unreliable and reliable network protocols in games?
options: Reliable is faster but unreliable is cheaper to operate
Unreliable (UDP) drops packets but is faster; reliable (TCP) guarantees delivery but adds latency. Games use unreliable for state, reliable for critical events.
Reliable is only for turn-based games
They have identical performance
questions.explanation: UDP state updates tolerate packet loss (next update overwrites old data); TCP critical messages (login, item pickup) must arrive. Mixing protocols lets games optimize for each use case.

questions.q: What does network jitter mean and how does it affect multiplayer games?
options: A lag compensation technique
Inconsistent latency between packets (some arrive in 50ms, others in 150ms), causing unpredictable animation stuttering and position corrections
The speed of light limit on network cables
A player's connection drop rate
questions.explanation: High jitter means latency variance is large. Even average latency of 100ms is tolerable; jitter of ±100ms creates unpredictable jumps as predictions are constantly corrected, making gameplay feel unstable.
